entry.title: Visualise end to end progress of Solution using Solution Quotation Fiori application
entry.content:
In today's fast-paced business environment, visibility and traceability are critical for effective decision-making. SAP’s Manage Solution Quotation Version 2 app addresses this need by offering a comprehensive view of the entire solution from creation of solution quotation to porgress of each follow-up documents execution. This blog highlighting the new feature and how businesses can leverage it to optimize operations.

From 2023 FPS01, new fiori app 'Manage Solution Quotations - Version 2' is availalbe to use. Using this app user can easily create new solution quotations or search or navigate to existing solution quotations. This app provides a network graph view, allowing user to track the status of a predefined set of follow-up documents such as sales orders, service orders, service contract or subscription contract. At each graphical node, like a sales order or subscription contract, you can see key details including the document ID and status with colour coded node of each follow-ups. A focused progress monitor is available at both the solution quotation header and item levels, offering a clear view of the overall process. Users can also navigate directly to specific documents or transactions, provided they have the necessary roles for access.

On open of this app you will see list of solution quotations created, user can search using the default filters, also can adapt the given filters.

To navigate to the details page of a solution quotation, click on the respective row. To visualize the end-to-end progress of the solution, user must click the "Action" button in the corresponding solution quotation row. On click on action button it opens the fiori object page with Details, Items and Progress Monitor. In this example I have used mixed bundled product which is a combination of different configurable non configurable products. Solution Quotation has been accepted which has generated its follow-up documents( sales order, service order, service contract and subscription contract).

By default, it shows the progress flow of the header documents as depicted in the image above. To visualize a specific item, click on the corresponding line item, To illustrate in this example I have selected item 40, which is a subscription item.

As you progress with specific documents, such as sales documents, you can visualize the further documents like outbound delivery and invoice. For example I have created billing documents for Subscription item and Service Contract, user can visualise end to end progress of header and items. 

Tips:The Progress Monitor UI offers useful controls in the right corner. Users can zoom in or zoom out, and clicking on 'Enter Full Screen' will expand the view for better visualization. Users can also highlight a particular document's flow by clicking on the document (node), as shown in the image below.

Another useful control is 'Expand All' and 'Collapse All.' As your document set grows, you can collapse all and expand only the documents you want to track. For example, here I have collapsed all documents and expanded the Service Contract flow.

Navigate to Documents from Progress Monitor: To navigate, the user can click on a node, which will display a set of buttons. By clicking the "Navigate to Document" button, the available navigation options(hyperlinks) will be shown depending on the user's authorizations. On click on hyperlink you can navigate from progress monitor to correponding documents. Similalry you can explore other interactive feature by clicking on button like Expand/Collapse, Node Details and Collapse Path.

Navigation of Sales Documents 

In summary, the SAP  Manage Solution Quotation version 2 app offers a user-friendly interface for managing and tracking solution quotations. The SAP solution quotation progress monitor enhances business efficiency by providing full visibility into the solution, enabling faster decision-making and improved collaboration. This streamlined process helps organizations reduce errors, accelerate decision making, and optimize resource allocation, driving overall business growth.

Stay tune for my next blog post I will try to explain the other features of Solution Business. I will be grateful to see your feedback on this blog post also let me know if you expect specific blog post on other offering of Manage Solution Quotation application or Solution Business.

entry.title: S/4 Hana Upgrades: Process of Cloning Tables in ZDO
entry.content:
The Process of Cloning Tables in ZDO

The process of creating table clones happens when the end-users have been rolled over to the bridge subsystem. Before the process of cloning can be started, Software Update Manager has to prepare the existing tables used by the source release (V1). The preparation includes renaming tables from the original name to a temporary name. This ensures that the target version (V2) of the table can be created with the original name.

The rename is called smart-switch and happens in the phase EU_SWITCH_ZDM. Here is a pseudo code describing what the phase EU_SWITCH_ZDM does:

  • Acquires an exclusive table lock for table tab1
  • Renames table tab1 to tab1temp
  • Releases the exclusive table lock for table tab1
  • Smart-switching tables require an exclusive lock for tables on database level. In case of frequently updated tables (approx. more than 10 changes / second), this can be difficult in peak load hours. Software Update Manager Toolbox offers the tool DB Table Lock analyser to identify suitable timeframes to plan the smart switch in.

Step 1 of the Process of Cloning Tables in ZDO

 

  • The initial transfer describes the process of creating the initial clone of the table with the same structure.
  • Software Update Manager executes the initial transfer in the phase SQLRUNTASK_FDCT_TRANSFER.
  • The transfer happens using native database tools.
  • For SAP HANA®, the tables are cloned with the statement CREATE TABLE tab(V2) LIKE tab(V1) WITH DATA.
  • From this time, all changes happening on version 1 of the table is captured in an associated logging table.
  • Capturing the changes in the logging table is happening by database triggers that have been created in the table version 1 before the initial transfer was started.

Step 2 of the Process of Cloning Tables in ZDO

 

  • The process of capturing changes in the logging table continues until the replay of the changes in phase RUN_ZDOREPLAY_REPLAY_LKPM starts.
  • Here, Software Update Manager switches the way how changes are captured.
  • The database trigger from table version 1 to the logging table stops capturing changes.
  • From this time, the changes done in table version 1 are synchronously forwarded to table version 2 by another database trigger.
  • All changes captured in the logging table are replayed into table version 2.
  • Replaying the changes and switching over to synchronously replication ensures transactional consistency.

Step 3 of the Process of Cloning Tables in ZDO

 

  • When the replay in the phase RUN_ZDOREPLAY_REPLAY_LKPM is finished, the logging table is no longer used.
  • The upgrade continues with the remaining activities such as adjusting the table version 2 to the target structure, importing table content, or executing application-defined procedures on table version 2.

entry.title: SAP Activate and Scaled Agile Framework Playbook is publicly available
entry.content:
The October 2024 release of the SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer, supporting the  SAP Activate Methodology for RISE with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition journey, introduces the SAP Activate and Scaled Agile Framework Playbook as an accelerator in the Prepare phase.

This playbook serves as a practical resource for teams embarking on S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition implementations in organizations utilizing the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) to enhance their agile deployment practices.

entry.title: Embrace the Change: Champion the Composable Enterprise
entry.content:
Unsure about your existing systems and lacking visibility into your business processes?

Don't know which solution to choose?

Overwhelmed by countless pages of system setup?

Facing lengthy implementation projects that drag on for months?

In today's rapidly evolving world, agility is essential for business success. However, the fear of disrupting existing systems and the challenges of adopting new solutions can hold you back. So, what can you do?

When you consider it, what companies truly need are business processes or capabilities. They aren't concerned with the underlying systems or how to connect them. Imagine if you could purchase these readily available capabilities on the market, plug them in, and compose your own processes. After purchasing, a simple one-click installation would have your processes up and running in minutes instead of months, all while keeping technical complexities hidden. If a particular capability doesn't meet your needs, you can easily remove it. Plus, you'll only pay for what you use through a subscription-based model. Additionally, you'll gain greater clarity on your processes and discover new ideas for improvement.

Does this sound like a dream? This is the essence of a Composable Enterprise. If you're eager to learn more and turn this vision into reality, join us on this exciting journey with SAP architects as we explore this strategic topic and work towards making future enterprises more composable. Sign up using the link below: 

Composable Enterprise: Reimagining Enterprise Software for Unprecedented Business Agility 

Customer registration for the project is open until November 22. Once registered, you'll receive an invitation to an introductory call.

entry.title: How SAP Business AI and Large Language Models are shaping the future of SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP
entry.content:
Terry Penner and Jürgen Butsmann discuss the latest developments in SAP Business AI for SAP S/4HANA and SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP).  The conversation focuses on how Artificial Intelligence is evolving at SAP, with particular attention to large language models (LLMs) and their growing application in SAP cloud ERP and SAP BTP.

Listen to the podcast: Spotify | Apple Podcasts

Topics discussed:

Part 1: Evolution of AI and Large Language Models (LLMs)

  • Latest developments in the AI market
  • SAP’s ERP strategy evolution for AI
  • Latest solution architecture updates

Part 2:  Differentiating the AI solutions in SAP cloud ERP

  • What is Joule
  • Differentiating AI offerings
  • How can AI add value to a business
  • Partner role with SAP Business AI

Part 3: Cloud ERP AI Priorities

  • SAP cloud ERP AI development priorities
  • SAP’s Business AI  scenario priorities

This blog is a follow-up to AI for SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP: deep-dive discussion

About the speakers:

Jürgen Butsmann is part of the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Product Marketing team responsible for AI in the context of SAP S/4HANA, finance and digital supply chain. Jürgen has been with SAP for more than 26 years.  He is a recognized expert for SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP .

Terry Penner is part of the SAP BTP marketing team for Business Applications, focused on SAP BTP for SAP S/4HANA. He has more than 23 years of technical and business experience in the SAP platform and analytics, including working directly on hundreds of customer implementations.

Part 1: Evolution of AI and Large Language Models (LLMs)

Latest developments in the AI market

Terry: In today's discussion, we'll explore how Artificial Intelligence for SAP S/4HANA and the SAP Business Technology Platform is evolving. Jürgen, what new developments have occurred in the AI market since we last spoke?

Jürgen: The evolution of technology continues unabated. We're observing an increase in services such as document services and advanced large language models (LLMs). The LLMs we see today are notably more fact-based, not just designed for entertainment purposes like the early iterations of ChatGPT, but for fact-oriented applications like those seen with Aleph Alpha. Additionally, there have been multiple iterations and diverse models emerging tailored to specific needs. We've also noticed a decline in pricing, which makes these AI services increasingly attractive and accessible in the market.

Terry: Absolutely. We've also witnessed significant legal developments, with content owners like The New York Times and others looking to withdraw their material from large language models, underscoring the importance of having robust and compliant data sources for these models.

SAP’s ERP strategy evolution for AI

Terry: How has SAP's ERP strategy for AI evolved?

Jürgen: Our strategy has evolved significantly, placing a more substantial emphasis on an AI-first approach across SAP. This shift means a lot of new ideas and pre-made implementations for our customers. We've taken a two-pronged approach in our investments. First, we're enhancing end-user productivity by integrating digital assistants into our systems, with Joule being a primary component. Although still early in deployment, these tools signify a shift from traditional graphical user interfaces to more intuitive digital assistant interactions.

Further confirming this direction, our CEO, Christian Klein, anticipates that in ten years, most system users will interact with digital assistants rather than traditional interfaces. Our focus also extends to natural language interactions within these systems, fostering intelligent activities and responses.

 

Secondly, we are concentrating on enhancing end-to-end processes. While we continue to enhance user interaction and productivity on the front end, significant efforts are directed towards optimizing large-scale processes, particularly within enterprise service management. This holistic approach ensures not just improvements in individual functionalities but also the acceleration of end-to-end process flows, all underpinned by advanced AI and generative AI technologies. This dual focus aligns with our commitment to transforming business processes through innovative AI applications.

Latest solution architecture updates

Terry: What are the latest updates to SAP's AI solution architecture since then?

Jürgen: One major change is the ongoing development of Joule. Initially, Joule had a rather rigid architecture that was not very flexible and did not support LLMs. This has changed. We have introduced the capability to create prompts using LLM support, which is a priority that has helped increase acceptance of this digital system and co-pilot for everyday work. Additionally, we've improved context sensitivity—knowing where you are in your system and what steps you are processing. This is a key differentiator from other co-pilots on the market and allows us to integrate responses that feel less artificial and more contextually aware not just to speak about integrating proprietary documents into the context of the Business Processes.

Furthermore, we continue to develop its extensibility. We've opened possibilities for customers to tailor the co-pilot to perform functions specific to their needs.

Terry: And regarding extensibility, that’s a significant area where SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) collaborates with the ERP team by supplying developer tools and experiences, such as the vector engine within SAP HANA, to support these functionalities—it's a joint effort.

Part 2:  Differentiating the AI solutions in SAP cloud ERP

What is Joule?

Terry: We’ve spoken quite a bit about Joule. Could you clarify exactly what Joule is?

Jürgen: Essentially, Joule serves as an interface for natural language commands within our system architecture. It acts as an entry point where users can interact with the system to trigger functions or retrieve necessary business process information. While Joule leverages AI technologies, its use doesn’t alter the inherent capabilities of our AI. It fits seamlessly into our system, making those use cases distinct and noteworthy. Ideally, Joule simplifies user interactions by providing a natural, conversational interface with the system. While our systems could operate without Joule, using it enhances the user experience, making interactions more engaging and straightforward.

  

Differentiating the AI solutions in SAP cloud ERP

Terry: What's the difference with SAP Business AI? How should customers differentiate potential AI offerings?

Jürgen: The top priority for SAP Business AI is to provide clear and measurable value to customers. Before using AI scenarios, there must be a reasonable and measurable return on investment. The focus is on implementing AI in SAP systems without the need to change generic functionality, where the system's context details can be used to address processes more effectively.

The reliability of AI use cases is a major focus, based on fact-based foundation models whenever possible. All AI-assisted processes undergo an ethical check and are supported by the data security of SAP Business AI architecture.

We are also continuing to refine our pricing approach and evaluating if we will continue using current AI units or shift to different technologies in the future. Customers consider usage, potential value add, costs, and investments when determining the break-even point for their use cases. Moreover, customers expect our AI solutions to be user-friendly.

Terry: Adding to that, not all AI use cases deliver sufficient value for application. It's crucial to concentrate on scenarios with high-quality underlying data linked to large language models. For example, one effective use of AI I've observed involves searching through support cases and providing recommendations to new team members, enhancing the search experience and improving how information is interpreted and suggested.

How can AI add value to a business

Terry: How would you explain to a customer how AI can add value to a business?

Jürgen: When considering how AI supports business processes, the user's connection to the system is paramount. Ideally, this happens through a digital co-pilot, like Joule in our case. Users interact with Joule by posing questions that prompt queries to a large language model, which in turn provides responses. However, large language models may lack specific business process details since those are unique and not universally predefined, leading to potentially incomplete answers. 

 

To address this, we might extend our architecture to incorporate calls to the ERP system or access other proprietary data, ensuring no sensitive business information is exposed to potentially vulnerable cloud environments. We balance accessibility with security by using a vector database to locate and integrate the right business-specific data from outside the large language model's scope.

As users submit human language requests, these are processed through the digital system and converted into technical prompts by a large language model. These prompts trigger the vector engine to retrieve the necessary information. The returning data can then be refined further by the large language models, which are all deployed securely within our system environment to prevent data leakage.

The data retrieval process benefits from enhancements such as semantic similarities and pattern recognition to continuously improve response accuracy. Once processed, the information is delivered back to the user, completing the cycle and delivering the value we aim for with our AI-enabled systems.

This systematic approach ensures that AI not only supports but also enhances business processes effectively while maintaining data integrity and security.

Terry: So, we could describe Joule as the front-end experience, distinct from the backend LLM that powers the AI functionalities.

Partner role with SAP Business AI

Terry: What role do partners play in this space?

Jürgen: Firstly, our partners have a deep understanding of our customers. It's crucial that partners are well-versed in our setup, strategy, architecture, pricing, and other elements that shape how we envision utilizing these services. We leverage our partners to create scalable solutions that precisely address specific customer needs. They are instrumental in implementing individual projects that tackle unique business challenges at the customer sites. Our partners have a proven track record of handling such use cases effectively.

Part 3: AI Extensibility and SAP BTP

SAP cloud ERP AI development priorities

Terry: What are some of the other AI development priorities at SAP cloud ERP, and how is SAP BTP supporting those AI scenarios?

 

Jürgen: We're integrating various technologies beyond what we've already discussed, like our work with live language models. For example, merging our capabilities with Microsoft Copilot offers expanded possibilities for enhancing natural language interactions. SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and the AI Foundation are pivotal in anchoring these flexibilities. This integration allows us to broaden our scope and reinforce the support we provide for cloud ERP AI scenarios.

SAP’s Business AI scenario priorities

Terry: What business scenarios are central to SAP's Business AI focus?

Jürgen: Our focus isn't narrow, but we do see significant interest particularly in the finance sector. Currently, we're engaging extensively with businesses, customers, and partners to pinpoint key priorities specific to finance. Our approach begins by assessing what immediate actions we can take and then contemplating more visionary possibilities for the future. For example, one idea is the automatic creation of forecast scenarios based on historical data within our systems. This capability could enhance management, strategy, and decision-making meetings significantly, even without all finance experts gathered at one table. Additionally, there are numerous other aspects of finance where AI can play a transformative role.

Terry: We will link to some specific use cases within the podcast notes. Thank you, Jürgen, for joining us and sharing your insights into AI for cloud ERP and the business technology platform. It's been enlightening.

Jürgen: Thank you for having me. I hope our discussion helps people understand better where we are now and where we are headed with our technology.

[ Note:  This blog was adapted from the podcast transcript by an SAP system using Azure OpenAI–gpt-4 ]

Key links:

  • Four ways to infuse business-ready, trusted AI into your processes
  • SAP BTP for S/4HANA
  • SAP S/4HANA
  • SAP Business AI

Read more in this series:

  • Artificial Intelligence (AI) for SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP: A deep dive
  • SAP S/4HANA Data Migration and Master Data Management Best Practices with SAP BTP
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Extensions with SAP Build Best Practices: An Expert Roundtable
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Extensions and Clean Core with SAP BTP - A Best Practices Conversation

entry.title: A Leap Forward in Encryption: TLS 1.3 Support in S/4HANA
entry.content:
TLS is a cryptographic protocol designed to provide secure communication over a computer network. It is used to establish a secure connection between a client and a server to ensure that the data exchanged is private and reliable.

The Benefits of TLS 1.3

The introduction of TLS1.3 in SAP S/4HANA 2023 brings several benefits designed to enhance security and streamline processes:

  1. Simplified and stronger cipher suites: TLS 1.3 removes support for outdated and potentially insecure algorithms and ciphers, focusing on a more streamlined and secure set. This simplification reduces the potential for misconfigurations and vulnerabilities while strengthening the overall security posture of your communications.
  2. Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) always enabled: With PFS enabled, each TLS session has a unique session key which cannot be recovered by learning the private key, ensuring that your session keys will not be compromised even if the server's private key is. In TLS1.3, PFS is always enabled, enhancing the confidentiality of your data.
  3. Simplified key exchange: TLS 1.3 only supports Diffie-Hellman type key exchange algorithms, removing support for RSA key exchange. This simplifies the key exchange process and eliminates potential vulnerabilities associated with RSA key exchange.

How to Configure TLS 1.3 in SAP S/4HANA OnPremise

Before configuring TLS 1.3, please make sure your system fits the version and patch requirements as detailed in SAP Note 3318423.

TLS versions can be configured via profile parameters. The following cipher suites are recommended by SAP for maximum interoperability, activating TLS 1.2 - 1.3 on the server (inbound) side, and TLS 1.0 - 1.3 on the client (outbound) side:

 Parameter NameParameter Value
Server (inbound)ssl/ciphersuites1569:PFS:HIGH::EC_X25519:EC_P256:EC_HIGH
Client (outbound)ssl/client_ciphersuites1174:PFS:HIGH::EC_X25519:EC_P256:EC_HIGH

As a note of caution: If your previous inbound configuration included TLS 1.0 - 1.1, please take a look at SAP Note 3346659 for how to safely restart your system after deactivating TLS 1.0 - 1.1.

If you prefer to have your inbound side open to TLS 1.0 - 1.1 as well as TLS 1.2 - 1.3, you can use the following parameter instead of the above one:

 Parameter NameParameter Value
Server (inbound)ssl/ciphersuites1159:PFS:HIGH::EC_X25519:EC_P256:EC_HIGH

If you would like to restrict your outbound communication to TLS 1.2 and 1.3 only, the following values are recommended:

 Parameter NameParameter Value
Client (outbound)ssl/client_ciphersuites1584:PFS:HIGH::EC_X25519:EC_P256:EC_HIGH

Please note that SAP currently does not recommend setting the cipher suite string parameter to TLS 1.2 and 1.3 for the outbound side, as this may cause interoperability issues when trying to communicate with older servers. Especially older embedded devices often do not support TLS 1.2 and upwards. Therefore, please verify that you have no incompatible servers in your system landscape before making such a change. You can read more about how to safely configure restrictive parameters for your system in SAP Note 3346659.

It is also possible to configure TLS 1.3 for specific clients in transaction sm59. For type G or H destinations, go to the tab "Logon & Security" and add your custom cipher suite string value in the field "TLS Cipher Suites".

This approach is especially recommended if you would like to activate TLS 1.3 systemwide, but have specific servers in your landscape which do not support TLS 1.3 and behave erroneously when a client tries to establish a TLS 1.3 connection. In this case, you can disable TLS 1.3 for these specific servers via the configuration shown in the above image.

Please also see the sm59 documentation for more details.

TLS 1.3 in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

TLS 1.3 is activated by default in all SAP S/4HANA Cloud Web Dispatchers. You can read more about it in this article.

Relevant SAP Notes for Further Information

SAP has released several SAP Notes explaining which software versions, patch levels, and configurations are required to enable TLS 1.3 for SAP S/4HANA:

3318423 - Is TLS 1.3 Supported by SAP Kernel for ABAP and SAP S/4HANA?
3346659 - TLS Ciphersuite String Configuration for ABAP and SAP Kernel-Based Systems

Conclusion

The support of TLS 1.3 is a significant leap forward for SAP S/4HANA 2023. It simplifies configurations, enhances security through stronger cipher suites, and ensures the safety of your communication through PFS and a simplified key exchange.

entry.title: Implement Currency Conversion in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Objective

Currency Conversion is an important aspect of any data modelling, planning, and reporting. SAP Datasphere software provides a standard functionality for Currency conversion. However, this is a quick step by step process on how currency conversion can be implemented with federation using S/4 HANA as a source. This article is completely based on my learning experience. I hope, this article will be useful for those consultants who are looking to capitalize the virtualization, federation, and tailor the needs based of the custom requirement.

How is it  different from standard approach?

1. This approach does not need all the currency relevant tables for the basic currency conversion but have the minimum four tables that are primarily involved as mentioned in the pre-requisites

2. Tables are federated or remote or virtual physical present in S/4 HANA. Unless there is a need for persistency can be scheduled at the input layer

3. Tables when remotely configured can be named according the naming conventions of the business need purposes

Prerequisites

  • Connection of SAP S/4 HANA on Premise or Cloud systems
  • Below standard tables activated in S/4 HANA systems

Table Name

Table Description

TCURV

 Exchange rate types

TCURX

 Decimal places in currencies

TCURR

 Exchange rates

TCURF

 Conversion factors

Option 1:

 Code snippet for SQL view

SELECT "FISCALYEARPERIOD", "PRODUCT","COMPANYCODE", "TRANSACTIONCURRENCY","AMOUNTINTRANSACTIONCURRENCY"

 

CONVERT_CURRENCY( AMOUNT => "AMOUNTINTRANSACTIONCURRENCY",

SOURCE_UNIT => "TRANSACTIONCURRENCY",

   "CONVERSION_TYPE" => 'M',

    "REFERENCE_DATE" => '2024-09-01', ----can be an input parameter

    "CLIENT" => '400',

    "SCHEMA" => 'DW_DEV_SCHEMA',

    "ERROR_HANDLING" => 'set_to_null',

    "TARGET_UNIT" => 'SAR', -----can be an input parameter

"PRECISIONS_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURX', --the table used for conversion must be declared here this is federated with remote table call

    "CONFIGURATION_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURV', --the table used for conversion must be declared here this is federated with remote table call

    "PREFACTORS_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURF', --the table used for conversion must be declared here this is federated with remote table call

    "RATES_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURR' --the table used for conversion must be declared here this is federated with remote table call

/***These remote tables when imported have been named with prefix as S4H in this case.

)

AS deb_cre_lc,

 

from "FIGL_LINEITEM" ----ACDOCA standard SAP Transaction Table

Resultset:

When converted from USD to SAR

Option 2:

Case of calculated column in a graphical view

Calculated column dbe_cre_lc definition

Code snippet

CONVERT_CURRENCY( AMOUNT => "AMOUNTINTRANSACTIONCURRENCY",

SOURCE_UNIT => "TRANSACTIONCURRENCY",

   "CONVERSION_TYPE" => 'M',

    "REFERENCE_DATE" => :IP_REFERENCE_DATE,

    "CLIENT" => '400',

    "SCHEMA" => ‘DW_DEV_SCHEMA',

    "ERROR_HANDLING" => 'set_to_null',

    "TARGET_UNIT" => :IP_TARGET_CURRENCY,

"PRECISIONS_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURX',---federated tables from S/4 HANA

"CONFIGURATION_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURV', ,---federated tables from S/4 HANA

"PREFACTORS_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURF', ,---federated tables from S/4 HANA

"RATES_TABLE" => 'S4H_TCURR') ,---federated tables from S/4 HANA

INPUT PARAMETERS

Resultset:
